name: Recursive DFS
summary: Depth of a node = 1 + the larger of its two subtree depths; recurse to the leaves and let the max bubble up.
visualizationIntent: init: build and show the input tree from the level-order array; every node idle. recurse: a cursor ring lands on the current node (node_id) and it turns amber (frame is on the call stack); DFS keeps descending toward the deepest leaf before any value is computed. return: as each call finishes, its node turns blue (fully returned) and its subtree depth (left_depth / right_depth) is known; the node keeps the larger of the two (best) and adds 1 (node_depth), so the deeper subtree's count bubbles up the tree. The longest amber root-to-leaf chain visible at peak recursion IS the max depth. done: the final answer scalar shows that depth.

lineExplanations.3: Count how many array slots describe the tree.
lineExplanations.4: Start an empty list of real node objects for the drawing.
lineExplanations.5: Start a lookup from a node's id to its object.
lineExplanations.6: Start a per-node state tracker: unseen, on-stack, or done.
lineExplanations.7: Loop over every slot to initialize its state.
lineExplanations.9: Point the child-slot walker just past the root.
lineExplanations.10: Loop over every slot to build the node objects.
lineExplanations.11: Only non-null slots become real nodes.
lineExplanations.12: Create this node with its value and two empty child links.
lineExplanations.13: Is there a slot left to be the left child?
lineExplanations.14: Is that candidate slot a real (non-null) node?
lineExplanations.15: Link that slot as this node's left child.
lineExplanations.16: Advance the walker past the left-child slot.
lineExplanations.17: Is there a slot left to be the right child?
lineExplanations.18: Is that candidate slot a real (non-null) node?
lineExplanations.19: Link that slot as this node's right child.
lineExplanations.20: Advance the walker past the right-child slot.
lineExplanations.21: Add the finished node to the tree.
lineExplanations.22: Record the node under its id for O(1) lookup.
lineExplanations.23: The root is slot 0, or nothing when the tree is empty.
lineExplanations.24: Recursively measure the tree's depth, starting at the root.
lineExplanations.25: Return the deepest path length as the answer.
lineExplanations.28: Base case: a missing node contributes zero depth.
lineExplanations.29: Return 0 for a null child — it adds no levels.
lineExplanations.30: Mark this node active — its frame is now on the call stack.
lineExplanations.31: Recurse left and remember how deep the left subtree runs.
lineExplanations.32: Recurse right and remember how deep the right subtree runs.
lineExplanations.33: Assume the left subtree is the deeper of the two.
lineExplanations.34: Is the right subtree actually deeper?
lineExplanations.35: If so, take the right subtree's depth instead.
lineExplanations.36: This node's depth is the deeper child plus one for itself.
lineExplanations.37: Both subtrees measured — mark this node fully done.
lineExplanations.38: Return this node's depth up to its parent.

syntaxExplanations.1: class Solution: the wrapper class LeetCode expects.
syntaxExplanations.2: def maxDepth(self, values): the entry method; values is the tree as a level-order array.
syntaxExplanations.3: len(values) counts the elements in the input array.
syntaxExplanations.5: by_id = {} creates an empty dictionary (a map from id to node).
syntaxExplanations.6: state_by_id = [] will hold one status number per node id.
syntaxExplanations.7: for i in range(n): loops i from 0 to n-1.
syntaxExplanations.8: append(0) adds a 0 (unseen) entry for this slot.
syntaxExplanations.9: child = 1 sets the next-child pointer to slot 1.
syntaxExplanations.10: for i in range(n): a second pass, this time to build nodes.
syntaxExplanations.11: is not None checks the slot holds a real value.
syntaxExplanations.12: A dict literal { ... } builds this node object.
syntaxExplanations.13: if child < n: is there still a slot available?
syntaxExplanations.14: Check the candidate child slot is not null.
syntaxExplanations.15: node["left"] = child stores the left child's id.
syntaxExplanations.16: child = child + 1 advances to the next slot.
syntaxExplanations.17: if child < n: is there another slot for the right child?
syntaxExplanations.18: Check the candidate right slot is not null.
syntaxExplanations.19: node["right"] = child stores the right child's id.
syntaxExplanations.21: nodes.append(node) adds the built node to the list.
syntaxExplanations.22: by_id[i] = node registers the node under its id.
syntaxExplanations.23: A ? B : C-style conditional: 0 if there is a root, else None.
syntaxExplanations.24: self.depth(...) calls the recursive helper on the root.
syntaxExplanations.25: return answer hands back the computed depth.
syntaxExplanations.27: def depth(self, node_id, nodes, by_id, state_by_id): the recursive helper; the extra args are passed so the visualization can see them each frame.
syntaxExplanations.28: if node_id is None: the base case — an absent child.
syntaxExplanations.29: return 0 exits this call with zero depth.
syntaxExplanations.30: state_by_id[node_id] = 1 marks the node active (on the stack).
syntaxExplanations.31: left_depth = self.depth(...) recurses into the left child.
syntaxExplanations.32: right_depth = self.depth(...) recurses into the right child.
syntaxExplanations.33: best = left_depth starts the maximum with the left depth.
syntaxExplanations.34: if right_depth > best: compares the two subtree depths.
syntaxExplanations.35: best = right_depth keeps the larger of the two.
syntaxExplanations.36: node_depth = best + 1 adds this node's own level.
syntaxExplanations.37: state_by_id[node_id] = 2 marks the node fully done.
syntaxExplanations.38: return node_depth ends this call, passing the depth up.
